title: Quiz: Photon Sources
description: Test your understanding of single-photon source technologies and their properties

questions.question: What conservation laws must be satisfied in spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC)?

options.text: Neither - SPDC is a spontaneous process

questions.explanation: SPDC must satisfy both energy conservation (omega_pump = omega_signal + omega_idler) and momentum conservation (k_pump = k_signal + k_idler). The momentum conservation condition is called phase matching and determines the output angles and wavelengths.

questions.question: In a heralded single-photon source using SPDC, what happens when you increase the pump power?

options.text: Brightness increases but multi-pair contamination also increases

questions.explanation: Higher pump power increases the pair generation rate (brightness) but also increases the probability of generating multiple pairs simultaneously. This multi-pair contamination degrades the single-photon purity, as g^(2)(0) increases with mean pair number.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of quantum dot single-photon sources over SPDC sources?
questions.explanation: Quantum dots provide deterministic single-photon emission - when triggered, they emit exactly one photon. SPDC is inherently probabilistic. However, QDs typically require cryogenic temperatures (4-20 K).

questions.question: What does g^(2)(0) = 0 indicate about a light source?

options.text: The source emits pairs of photons
The source is a perfect single-photon source (complete antibunching)
The source is a coherent laser

questions.explanation: g^(2)(0) = 0 indicates perfect photon antibunching - no two photons are ever detected simultaneously. This is the signature of an ideal single-photon source. Coherent light has g^(2)(0) = 1, and thermal light has g^(2)(0) = 2.

questions.question: Why is resonance fluorescence preferred over above-band excitation for quantum dot single-photon sources?

options.text: It produces photons with higher indistinguishability and no timing jitter
It produces photon pairs instead of single photons

questions.explanation: Resonance fluorescence directly excites the QD transition, producing photons that are highly indistinguishable with minimal timing jitter. Above-band excitation creates carriers that must relax to the emitting state, introducing dephasing and timing uncertainty.

questions.question: The Hong-Ou-Mandel (HOM) visibility directly measures the indistinguishability of photons from a single-photon source.
questions.explanation: True! The HOM visibility quantifies how identical two photons are. When two indistinguishable photons meet at a beam splitter, they always exit together (no coincidences). Any distinguishability reduces this effect, giving a visibility less than 1.

questions.question: What is the purpose of multiplexing in single-photon sources?

options.text: To increase the effective determinism of probabilistic sources
To cool the source to cryogenic temperatures

questions.explanation: Multiplexing combines many probabilistic sources with active switching. If each source has probability p of producing a photon, N multiplexed sources give probability 1-(1-p)^N, approaching 1 as N increases. This converts a probabilistic source into a quasi-deterministic one.

questions.question: Which type of SPDC phase matching produces photon pairs with orthogonal polarizations?
questions.explanation: Type-II SPDC produces signal and idler photons with orthogonal polarizations. Type-I produces both photons with the same polarization (opposite to pump), and Type-0 produces both with the same polarization as the pump.
